Contrast: Request for quotation — a request for proposal seeks a proposed solution and approach; a request for quotation mainly seeks price for a clearly specified need.
Example: A public agency issues a request for proposal for a digital permitting platform and compares vendors on implementation approach, support model, and delivery capability.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, request for proposal

Core Meaning: A procurement document used to solicit price quotations from prospective sellers for a defined product, service, or result.
Expansion: A request for quotation is used when the buyer can define the requirement clearly and wants sellers to compete primarily on price and commercial terms. It is best suited to standardized goods or well-specified services. PMI treats it as a formal procurement solicitation document for obtaining comparable quotations.
Contrast: Request for bid — a request for quotation asks for quotations against a defined requirement; a request for bid is a closely related solicitation term often used where formal bid competition is emphasized.
Example: A project team sends a request for quotation for backup servers with fixed technical specifications and compares supplier pricing and delivery dates.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, request for quotation

Core Meaning: A procurement document used to obtain price quotations from sellers for a well-defined requirement.
Expansion: A request for quote is used when the buyer can specify the needed product, service, or result clearly enough that suppliers mainly compete on price and delivery terms. It is most suitable for standardised or tightly specified purchases rather than situations requiring sellers to propose substantially different solution approaches. In PMI procurement practice, it is one of the common bidder conference and solicitation-related instruments used in conducting procurements.
Contrast: Request for proposal — a request for quote seeks pricing for a defined need, while a request for proposal seeks a proposed solution and approach.
Example: A project team issues a request for quote for 200 laptops with fixed technical specifications and compares supplier responses on cost and lead time.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, Request for quote, page 248

Core Meaning: The sellers chosen, after evaluation, to provide contracted products or services.
Expansion: Selected sellers are the vendors judged to be in a competitive range based on proposal evaluation and with whom the buyer is prepared to negotiate or has awarded a contract. They are an output of conducting procurements.
Contrast: Prospective sellers - prospective sellers are all candidates invited to bid, whereas selected sellers are those chosen after evaluation.
Example: After scoring three vendor proposals, the buyer names the top vendor as the selected seller and awards the contract.
Source: PMBOK Guide - selected sellers

Core Meaning: Selection criteria are the attributes or requirements used to evaluate and choose among alternatives such as sellers, projects, solutions, or response options.
Expansion: In PMI practice, selection criteria provide a structured basis for comparing options against agreed decision factors instead of relying on informal preference. They are commonly used in procurement, portfolio and project selection, and decision analysis where alternatives must be assessed consistently. Typical criteria include capability, cost, schedule, risk, quality, compliance, and strategic alignment.
Contrast: Evaluation method — selection criteria are the factors used to judge alternatives, while an evaluation method is the technique used to apply those factors and compare options.
Example: A project team evaluating vendors for a cloud migration scores proposals against security capability, implementation timeline, support model, transition risk, and functional fit.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A provider of products, services, or results to a buyer under a procurement arrangement.
Expansion: A seller is the party that provides contracted products, services, or results to the buyer. In project work, the seller is typically an external vendor, contractor, or supplier engaged through procurement.
Contrast: Buyer — the seller provides the product or service, while the buyer acquires it.
Example: A cloud hosting company is the seller when it contracts to provide infrastructure services for a platform migration project.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, seller, page 24

Core Meaning: A structured review of a contracted seller's delivery, quality, and compliance against the agreed terms of the contract.
Expansion: Seller performance evaluation is part of controlling procurements, where the buyer assesses how well a vendor meets cost, schedule, quality, and scope obligations. Findings inform decisions about payments, corrective action, contract continuation, and the seller's eligibility for future work. It typically draws on inspections, audits, and procurement performance reviews documented across the contract life cycle.
Contrast: Differs from source selection, which evaluates sellers before award; seller performance evaluation judges actual delivery after award.
Example: A project manager rates a software vendor each quarter on defect rates and on-time releases, using the scores to justify renewing the support contract.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: Responses prepared by prospective sellers that describe how they will meet a buyer's procurement requirements.
Expansion: Seller proposals are submitted in reply to procurement documents such as requests for proposal or similar bid packages. In PMI practice, they are evaluated to support source selection by comparing technical, management, and commercial responses against defined criteria. They are a key input to selecting vendors and forming procurement agreements.
Contrast: Bid documents — seller proposals are the vendors' responses, while bid documents are the buyer's solicitation materials used to request those responses.
Example: A public-sector programme issues an RFP for a cloud platform and evaluates seller proposals on security architecture, delivery capability, and implementation approach.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, page 498

Core Meaning: A documented agreement that defines the expected level, scope, and quality of a service, often including measurable performance targets and responsibilities.
Expansion: In project and operational contexts, a service-level agreement establishes what service will be provided, how performance will be measured, and what each party is accountable for. PMI materials commonly treat it as a governance and procurement-related mechanism for clarifying expectations between service providers and customers or internal support groups. It is especially relevant in outsourced services, support transitions, and projects that hand off deliverables into operations.
Contrast: Contract — a service-level agreement defines specific service expectations and performance measures; a contract is the broader legally binding agreement that may contain or reference an SLA.
Example: A cloud-migration project includes an SLA requiring the hosting provider to meet agreed uptime and incident-response targets once the new platform goes live.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The agreed split between buyer and seller of cost savings or overruns under an incentive contract.
Expansion: A share ratio, often written as a buyer or seller percentage such as 80/20, determines how a difference between target and actual cost is divided. It motivates the seller to control costs because they keep a portion of any savings and bear a portion of any overrun.
Contrast: Differs from a fixed fee, which does not change with cost; the share ratio deliberately ties seller profit to cost performance.
Example: Under an 80/20 share ratio, a 10,000 underrun returns 8,000 to the buyer and 2,000 to the seller as added fee.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: The agreed split of cost savings or overruns between buyer and seller in an incentive contract.
Expansion: In incentive-fee contracts, the sharing ratio defines how a difference between target cost and actual cost is divided between buyer and seller, written as buyer:seller (for example 80:20). It motivates the seller to control costs because they keep a share of any savings and absorb a share of any overrun. The ratio is central to calculating final fee and the point of total assumption in fixed-price incentive contracts.
Contrast: Point of Total Assumption - the sharing ratio sets how over/underruns are split, whereas the point of total assumption is the cost level above which the seller bears all additional cost.
Example: With an 80:20 sharing ratio, a 10,000 dollar underrun gives the buyer 8,000 dollars and the seller 2,000 dollars.
Source: PMBOK Guide - incentive contracts / share ratio

Core Meaning: The process of obtaining bids, proposals, or quotations from prospective sellers for project procurement work.
Expansion: Solicitation is the procurement activity in which the buyer invites qualified sellers to respond to defined project needs. It typically uses documents such as invitations for bid, requests for proposal, or requests for quotation. In PMI usage, it sits between procurement planning and source selection, creating the formal basis for comparing seller responses.
Contrast: Source selection — solicitation requests and receives seller responses; source selection evaluates them and chooses the seller.
Example: A project team issues an RFP for a cybersecurity platform and receives proposals from five vendors for evaluation.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Solicitation

Core Meaning: The process of evaluating seller proposals or bids and choosing the supplier most suitable for the project's procurement needs.
Expansion: Source selection is a procurement concept used when a project team compares potential sellers against predefined criteria to choose a supplier. The emphasis is not only on price, but also on capability, risk, quality, schedule fit, and contractual considerations. It helps ensure procurement decisions are transparent, defensible, and aligned to project objectives.
Contrast: Bidder conference — a bidder conference communicates procurement information to prospective sellers; source selection is the later step of evaluating responses and choosing among them.
Example: A project team issues an RFP for a data-migration vendor, scores proposals against technical competence, delivery timeline, and support model, and selects the supplier with the strongest overall fit.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), procurement concept; exact page citation not confirmed in available PMI search results

Core Meaning: A technique for choosing the method and criteria used to evaluate and select sellers.
Expansion: Source selection analysis reviews how proposals will be weighed before solicitation, balancing factors such as price, quality, technical capability, and risk. It produces a selection method such as least cost, qualifications only, quality-based, or fixed budget. The chosen approach shapes the evaluation criteria included in procurement documents.
Contrast: Unlike make-or-buy analysis, which decides whether to procure at all, source selection analysis decides how to pick among competing sellers once buying is chosen.
Example: For a complex IT build, the team uses a quality-based method so technical merit outweighs the lowest bid.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A set of attributes desired by the buyer that a seller is evaluated against for contract award.
Expansion: Source selection criteria are the standards used to assess seller proposals during procurement. They can include technical capability, relevant experience, management approach, risk profile, and cost-related considerations. PMI treats them as a structured basis for comparing offers and selecting the most suitable seller.
Contrast: Bid documents — source selection criteria are the standards used to evaluate sellers, while bid documents are the materials issued to solicit and structure seller responses.
Example: A public-sector cybersecurity project scores vendors against source selection criteria including incident response capability, regulatory experience, transition approach, and service continuity.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition, source selection criteria, page 471

Core Meaning: A narrative description of products, services, or results to be delivered by the project.
Expansion: A statement of work, or SOW, describes what is to be delivered and often provides the basis for procurement or project authorisation. It may include scope, deliverables, timing, and other expectations depending on the context. PMI uses it as a key input for defining and contracting work.
Contrast: Scope statement — a statement of work is a narrative description of requested work or deliverables; a scope statement is the project document that defines the project and product scope in more detail.
Example: A vendor SOW for a website redesign specifies the pages, content migration, testing support, and delivery milestones expected from the supplier.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — statement of work

Core Meaning: A contract under which a primary contractor delegates part of its contracted work to another party.
Expansion: A subcontract is an agreement in which a prime contractor engages a third party, the subcontractor, to perform a defined portion of the work the prime owes to the buyer. It lets the prime contractor obtain specialized capability or capacity while remaining accountable to the buyer for the whole scope. Managing subcontracts adds a layer of coordination, flow-down requirements, and risk that the prime must control.
Contrast: The prime contract binds the buyer and main contractor, while a subcontract binds the main contractor and a lower-tier supplier.
Example: A general contractor subcontracts the electrical scope to a specialist firm while remaining responsible to the client.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A seller engaged by a prime contractor to perform part of the work the prime has contracted to deliver.
Expansion: A subcontractor holds a contract with the prime contractor rather than directly with the buyer, and the prime remains accountable for the overall result. Managing subcontractors involves flow-down of requirements, quality control, and coordination of interfaces.
Contrast: Differs from a prime contractor, who is directly accountable to the buyer; a subcontractor answers to the prime.
Example: The main builder hires an electrical subcontractor for wiring while remaining responsible to the client for the whole building.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: An external party that provides products, services, or results needed by a project, program, or portfolio.
Expansion: A supplier is the organisation or party that furnishes contracted goods or services to support delivery. In PMI usage, suppliers are external stakeholders whose performance can directly affect quality, schedule, cost, and risk.
Contrast: Seller — supplier is the broader external source of goods or services, while seller is the contracting party in a specific buyer-seller relationship.
Example: A cloud-platform provider acts as the supplier for a migration project that depends on hosted infrastructure and support services.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, supplier; exact page varies by edition formatting

Core Meaning: The end-to-end network involved in sourcing, producing, moving, and delivering materials, components, or services.
Expansion: A supply chain links the organisations, flows, and dependencies that move needed inputs from source to point of use. In projects, it matters because delays, shortages, transport issues, or quality failures anywhere in that network can affect delivery outcomes. It is especially relevant in construction, manufacturing, infrastructure, and any project relying on external materials or specialised vendors.
Contrast: Logistics — supply chain is the broader network of sourcing and delivery relationships, while logistics focuses on the movement and storage of items within that network.
Example: A hospital-build project manages the supply chain for steel, chillers, medical gas systems, and imported imaging equipment to prevent installation delays.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The negotiated reference price under an incentive contract, used to share cost variances.
Expansion: Target price is the agreed reference price in an incentive contract, derived from target cost plus target fee. The final price is adjusted from it using a sharing ratio that splits cost overruns or underruns between buyer and seller.
Contrast: Distinct from a firm fixed price, which never changes, target price moves within limits based on actual performance against the target cost.
Example: With a target cost of 100k and a fee of 10k, the target price is 110k before applying the sharing ratio.
Source: PMBOK Guide

Core Meaning: A contract instrument that authorizes a discrete package of services under the terms of an existing indefinite-delivery indefinite-quantity master contract.
Expansion: A task order defines the scope, period of performance, and funding for a specific tranche of labor or services without renegotiating the underlying master agreement. It lets buyers issue work incrementally as needs emerge while pricing and terms stay fixed at the master-contract level. Task orders are common in government and large professional-services procurements. Each order can carry its own deliverables and acceptance criteria.
Contrast: Delivery order — a delivery order procures supplies or goods under the same master contract, whereas a task order procures services or labor.
Example: A program office issues a task order against its engineering master contract to staff a six-month requirements study.
Source: Procurement management practice (indefinite-delivery contracting)

Core Meaning: A time-and-materials contract is a type of contract that pays the seller for actual labor time and materials used, typically with agreed rates and terms.
Expansion: In project management, a time-and-materials contract blends features of fixed-price and cost-reimbursable arrangements by compensating the seller for actual effort and materials consumed. It is often used when the scope cannot be fully defined at the outset but work must begin before all details are known. Because cost can grow if the work expands, buyers usually pair it with rate schedules, oversight, and sometimes not-to-exceed limits.
Contrast: Cost-reimbursable contract — a time-and-materials contract pays agreed labor rates plus materials, while cost-reimbursable arrangements reimburse allowable actual costs with a fee structure.
Example: A company hires a specialist integration vendor at agreed hourly rates plus hardware pass-through costs to support a system rollout while final interface requirements are still emerging.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide, procurement contract types entry; exact page citation requires source verification

Core Meaning: An external supplier that provides goods or services to the project or organization.
Expansion: A vendor is a party outside the performing organization that supplies products, services, or both. In project management, vendors are central to procurement, contract administration, performance monitoring, and acceptance of delivered outputs. PMI usage overlaps with related terms such as seller and supplier, though context may make one more precise than another.
Contrast: Seller — vendor is a broad term for an external provider; seller is the procurement term more directly tied to an offer or contract to provide goods or services.
Example: A project team engages a vendor to provide cloud hosting and implementation support for a new customer portal.
Source: PMI Lexicon, vendor, exact page not confirmed

Core Meaning: A contractual promise by a seller that a deliverable will meet specified standards or perform as agreed for a defined period.
Expansion: A warranty is an assurance embedded in a contract that goods, services, or work will conform to stated requirements and remain fit for purpose over a warranty period. If the deliverable fails within that period, the warranty obligates the seller to repair, replace, or otherwise remedy the defect, often at no cost to the buyer. Warranties allocate post-delivery quality risk to the seller and are a key term negotiated in procurement.
Contrast: A warranty is a specific contractual condition about performance, while a guarantee is a broader assurance that may be informal or promotional.
Example: A construction contract includes a one-year warranty obliging the contractor to fix structural defects discovered after handover.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A method of assigning relative importance to evaluation criteria so options can be scored and compared objectively.
Expansion: A weighting system applies numeric weights to each selection or evaluation criterion according to its importance, scores each option against the criteria, and combines the results into a weighted total. It is widely used in project selection and source selection to make multi-criteria decisions consistent and defensible. By making priorities explicit, it reduces the influence of bias and of any single dominant factor.
Contrast: A screening system applies pass-or-fail thresholds, while a weighting system produces comparative scores.
Example: A committee scores three vendors on cost, quality, and delivery using assigned weights to choose the best overall fit.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Sixth Edition
